title: exp-004-a3: compliant insertion with force feedback
description:
## Hypothesis
Once aligned with the port (from A1 or A2), the insertion phase requires
compliance. Low stiffness + force monitoring should allow the connector
to slide into the port without excessive force. A spiral search pattern
can compensate for small alignment errors.

## Branch
A3 (classical control, force-feedback insertion)

## Changes Planned
- After approach phase, switch to low stiffness
- Monitor wrench for contact detection
- Implement spiral search if initial push fails
- Gradual Z descent with XY compliance

## Expected Outcome
- Tier 3: 50-75 (partial to full insertion)
- No force penalties

title: exp-003-a2: camera-based port detection
description:
## Hypothesis
The 3 wrist cameras (left, center, right) at 1152x1024 provide enough
visual information to detect the target port. If we can estimate port
pose from images, we can servo toward it without ground truth.

## Branch
A2 (classical control, vision-based)

## Changes Planned
- Capture camera images from observations
- Try template matching / feature detection for port location
- Estimate port pose relative to camera frame
- Command robot to align with detected port

## Expected Outcome
- Port detection working -> proximity points
- Depends on A1 results showing approach strategy works

title: methodology: design iterative experiment loop
description:
## Context
We need a formal methodology for the AIC hackathon that turns experimentation
into a systematic loop. The harness should be able to run itself -- trying
approaches, scoring them, logging results, and adjusting.

## Task
1. Determine how local scoring works (aic_engine + Gazebo)
2. Design the experiment loop: propose -> implement -> score -> log -> adjust
3. Write methodology to .claude/refs/methodology.md
4. Codify into a /experiment skill
5. Set up beads as research log (each experiment = bead with results)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] methodology.md written with formal experiment protocol
- [ ] /experiment skill created
- [ ] Beads structure defined for research logging
- [ ] Clear pathway from "idea" to "scored result" to "next idea"

title: human: set up OVH L4-90 cloud instance
description:
## Steps to Set Up OVH L4-90 Cloud GPU Instance

### 1. Create the Instance on OVH

1. Go to https://us.ovhcloud.com/public-cloud/prices/
2. Create a Public Cloud project (if you don't have one)
3. Launch an instance:
   - **Flavor**: L4-90 ($1.00/hr)
   - **Region**: Pick closest to you (US-EAST for lowest latency to AWS ECR)
   - **OS**: Ubuntu 24.04
   - **SSH Key**: Add your public key during creation
     - If you need to generate one: `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`
     - Copy that into the OVH SSH key field
   - **Name**: something like `aic-gpu`
4. Note the public IP address once it's running

### 2. On THIS machine: Add SSH config

```bash
cat >> ~/.ssh/config << 'EOF2'

Host gpu
    HostName <ovh-instance-ip>
    User ubuntu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    ForwardAgent yes
EOF2
```

Replace `<ovh-instance-ip>` with the actual IP.

Test: `ssh gpu echo "connected"`

### 3. On THIS machine: Run the setup script on the GPU instance

```bash
scp scripts/gpu-setup.sh gpu:~/gpu-setup.sh
ssh gpu "chmod +x ~/gpu-setup.sh && ~/gpu-setup.sh"
```

This takes ~10-15 min. It installs:
- Pixi + ROS 2 Kilted + Gazebo
- Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit
- Creates ~/run-eval.sh
- Clones the repo (or prompts you to)
- Verifies GPU, Gazebo, ROS 2, Docker all work

### 4. Update runner config

Edit `scripts/runner-config.sh` on this machine:
```bash
GPU_HOST="gpu"
USE_REMOTE_EVAL="true"
```

### 5. Test end-to-end

```bash
scripts/remote-eval.sh aic_example_policies.ros.WaveArm
```

Should take ~5 min (vs 27 min locally). Expect WaveArm to score ~1/trial.

### 6. Run our first real experiment

```bash
scripts/remote-eval.sh aic_example_policies.ros.BlindPush
```

### 7. When done for the day

Stop the instance from OVH console to stop billing.
Storage persists -- next time just start it and SSH in.

## Cost Notes
- $1.00/hr while running
- Stop when not using it
- Storage persists when stopped (pennies/day)
- Budget ~$250 for entire qualification phase

title: cleanup: prune Docker images and reclaim disk space
description:
## Context
Docker images and build cache consume significant disk space on this machine.
After migrating eval to the Mac Studio, we can reclaim this space.

## Task
1. Stop any running containers: `docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yaml down`
2. Report current disk usage:
   - `docker system df -v`
   - `df -h /`
3. Prune everything:
   - `docker system prune -a --volumes` (removes all images, containers, volumes, build cache)
4. Report reclaimed space:
   - `docker system df -v` (should show ~0)
   - `df -h /` (compare before/after)
5. Provide summary report:
   - Space before cleanup
   - Space after cleanup
   - Space reclaimed
   - Remaining Docker footprint (if any)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All unused Docker images removed
- [ ] Build cache cleared
- [ ] Report showing before/after disk usage
- [ ] At least 40GB reclaimed (eval 9.5GB + model 13.7GB + cache 34GB)

## Notes
- Do NOT run this until the Mac runner is confirmed working
- The eval image would need to be re-pulled (~10 min) if we ever need local Docker again
- The model image rebuild would take ~5 min

title: exp-005-b1: collect CheatCode demonstrations for ACT training
spec: Mac Studio runner architecture and setup script
impl: update all skills/docs for cloud runner
infra: Mac Studio remote runner setup
description:
## Context
Current CPU-only eval takes ~27 min per run. Mac Studio M1 Max with Metal GPU
could cut this to ~10-15 min with native Gazebo. Need SSH-accessible remote runner.

## Task
1. Create Mac setup script (native macOS, not Docker)
2. Set up SSH-based experiment execution
3. Wire results back to this machine for analysis

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Setup script for Mac Studio (Homebrew + Gazebo + ROS2 + Pixi)
- [ ] Remote run script (SSH + rsync policy code + run eval + fetch results)
- [ ] Skills and docs updated for remote runner flow
- [ ] End-to-end test: run experiment on Mac, get scoring.yaml here

title: exp-006-c1: hybrid vision approach + classical insertion
infra: docker setup for headless CPU-only eval
human: set up Mac Studio as remote eval runner
description:
## Steps to Set Up Your Mac Studio M1 Max as the Primary Dev + Submission Machine

### 1. On THIS machine (dev host): Generate SSH key

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "aic-runner" -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
# Press enter for no passphrase (or set one)
```

### 2. On the Mac Studio: Enable Remote Login

1. System Settings → General → Sharing
2. Enable Remote Login (SSH)
3. Note the IP address (e.g., 192.168.1.50) or hostname (mac-studio.local)

### 3. On THIS machine: Copy SSH key to Mac

```bash
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 <username>@<mac_ip>
# Test: ssh <username>@<mac_ip> echo "SSH works"
```

### 4. On THIS machine: Add SSH config

```bash
cat >> ~/.ssh/config << 'EOF2'

Host mac
    HostName <mac_ip_or_hostname>
    User <username>
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    ForwardAgent yes
EOF2
```

Test: `ssh mac echo "config works"`

### 5. On the Mac: Install Xcode CLI Tools

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

### 6. On the Mac: Install Homebrew

```bash
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

### 7. On the Mac: Install Docker Desktop

Download from https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/
(needed for final submission builds only, not for iteration)

### 8. From THIS machine: Run setup script on Mac

```bash
scp scripts/mac-setup.sh mac:~/mac-setup.sh
ssh mac "chmod +x ~/mac-setup.sh && ~/mac-setup.sh"
```

Takes ~10-20 min. Installs Pixi, ROS 2, Gazebo, creates ~/run-eval.sh.

### 9. Update runner config

Edit scripts/runner-config.sh: set MAC_HOST="mac"

### 10. Test end-to-end

```bash
scripts/remote-eval.sh aic_example_policies.ros.WaveArm
```

Should rsync code, run eval on Mac, fetch scoring.yaml back.

## Architecture

Mac Studio = everything:
- Native pixi + Gazebo for fast iteration (~10 min eval)
- Docker Desktop for submission builds (one-shot, not iteration)
- This machine = code editing + orchestration via SSH

title: exp-001-a0: get Tier 1 passing with minimal policy
description:
## Hypothesis
A minimal policy that extends aic_model.Policy, implements insert_cable(),
and sends at least one valid MotionUpdate should pass Tier 1 (1 point) and
potentially score proximity points if it moves toward the port at all.

## Branch
A0 (pre-branch baseline)

## Changes Planned
- Create our own policy package directory
- Implement simplest possible Policy subclass
- Send a single Cartesian command to current TCP position
- Verify Tier 1 passes

## Expected Outcome
- Tier 1: 1 (pass)
- Tier 2: 0 (no meaningful motion)
- Tier 3: 0-10 (might get proximity if starting position is close)
- Total: 1-11 per trial, 3-33 total

title: infra: pivot to OVH cloud L4 GPU runner
impl: update skills/CLAUDE.md for remote runner flow
exp-002-a1: move toward port using task TF data
description:
## Hypothesis
During training (ground_truth:=true), TF frames for ports are published.
We can use controller_state.tcp_pose to know where we are, and compute a
direction vector from TCP to port. Moving along that vector should get us
proximity points (0-25) and possibly partial insertion.

NOTE: TF is NOT available during eval. This experiment validates whether
simple approach-toward-port works, before we invest in camera perception.

## Branch
A1 (classical control, ground-truth bootstrap)

## Changes Planned
- Look up port TF frame during insert_cable
- Compute vector from TCP to port
- Send Cartesian position commands stepping toward port
- Log distance over time

## Expected Outcome
- Tier 3: 25-75 (proximity to full insertion)
- Provides upper bound for classical approach
